questions.stem: In Python, what is a variable?
options: A fixed value that cannot change during program execution
A name that refers (points) to an object stored in memory — reassigning the name makes it point to a different object
A reserved word in the Python language
A container that can only hold numbers
questions.explanation: Python variables are references (labels) to objects. x = 5 creates an integer object 5 and makes x point to it. x = 'hello' makes x point to a string instead — the integer 5 still exists until garbage collected. This is why y = x means both names point to the SAME object: modifying a mutable object via y also affects x. Understanding this prevents subtle bugs with lists and tensors.

questions.stem: What is the difference between mutable and immutable objects in Python?
options: Mutable objects are stored in RAM; immutable objects are stored on disk
Mutable objects can be changed after creation (lists, dicts, tensors); immutable objects cannot (int, str, tuple) — modifying an immutable object creates a new one
Mutable objects can hold any type; immutable objects can only hold numbers
Mutable means the variable name can change; immutable means the name is fixed
questions.explanation: Mutable: a = [1, 2, 3]; a.append(4) modifies the same list object. Immutable: s = 'hello'; s.upper() returns a NEW string. In ML: torch.Tensor is mutable (in-place ops like tensor.add_(1) modify it directly); Python int/float/str are immutable. Gotcha: using in-place operations on requires_grad tensors breaks autograd computation graphs.

questions.stem: What does loss.item() do in PyTorch and why is it important?
options: Moves the loss tensor from GPU to CPU
Extracts the Python scalar value from a single-element tensor, detaching it from the computation graph — prevents the training loop from accumulating the entire gradient graph in memory
Computes the gradient of the loss with respect to all parameters
Resets the loss to zero for the next batch
questions.explanation: loss is a PyTorch tensor that carries a computation graph (to enable backprop). If you store loss in a list, you're storing the GRAPH too. After thousands of batches, this consumes gigabytes. loss.item() extracts a plain Python float — no graph attached. Critical pattern: losses.append(loss.item()), NOT losses.append(loss). The same applies to any metric you log during training.

questions.stem: What is type annotation in Python (e.g. def train(model: nn.Module, lr: float = 0.001) -> None:)?
options: Runtime enforcement that raises an error if the wrong type is passed
Documentation for humans and tools (like mypy and IDEs) that describes expected types — not enforced by Python at runtime but enables static checking and autocomplete
A way to convert variables to the specified type automatically
Required syntax for Python 3.10+ — code won't run without it
questions.explanation: Python type hints are metadata — they don't change runtime behaviour. def f(x: int) still accepts a string at runtime. The value: (1) IDEs can autocomplete and detect bugs before running, (2) mypy performs static type checking to catch type errors in a whole codebase, (3) they serve as machine-readable documentation. In large ML codebases, type hints prevent entire classes of bugs (wrong tensor shapes, wrong argument types).

questions.stem:
Why does the following code print 'hello' instead of 'world'?

x = 'hello'
y = x
x = 'world'
print(y)
options: Python has a bug in string assignment
y = x copies the reference at that moment; x = 'world' makes x point to a new string, but y still points to 'hello' — they're independent references after reassignment
Strings in Python are passed by value, so y gets a copy immediately
x = 'world' is invalid syntax and Python silently ignores it
questions.explanation: When y = x executes, both x and y point to the string 'hello'. Then x = 'world' makes x point to a new string object — but y is unchanged. y still points to 'hello'. This is reference semantics: assignment changes where a name points, not the object itself. Contrast with mutable objects: a = [1,2]; b = a; a.append(3); print(b) → [1,2,3] because both names point to the SAME list and .append modifies it in place.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of Python's None, and how does it differ from 0 or False?
options: None is equivalent to 0 — it's just a different way to represent zero
None is a special singleton object representing the ABSENCE of a value — semantically different from 0 (a number), False (a boolean), or '' (an empty string)
None means the variable has been deleted from memory
None is Python's version of null that causes errors when accessed
questions.explanation: None represents 'no value' or 'not yet set'. 0 is a valid integer; False is a valid boolean; '' is a valid empty string. None is the absence of any of these. In ML: functions that don't explicitly return something return None; optional parameters default to None (def forward(self, mask=None)); checking 'if mask is not None' is idiomatic Python for optional arguments. Never use == None — use 'is None' (None is a singleton).
